reasoning:
Looking at this problem through the lens of "unimpatient" — a word suggesting active readiness rather than passive waiting — I'm struck by a fundamental assumption we might flip: retirement systems wait for YOU to have money before they start working. What if the system moved first?

The core insight: For irregular earners, the tragedy isn't missed contributions — it's missed compound time. Every month you can't contribute is growth you never capture. Traditional systems are "patient" in the worst way: they wait politely while your future evaporates.

```json
{
  "label": "Advance Retirement Accounts (ARAs)",
  "design_principles": "The system contributes first; you repay when able. Inconsistency expected, not penalized. Capture compound time regardless of cash flow. Separate contribution timing from growth timing entirely.",
  "core_mechanism": "Government continuously funds your retirement account at a base rate. When you earn above a threshold, income-linked deductions 'repay' advances. You keep all market gains; they recover only inflation-adjusted principal.",
  "how_it_works": "Every adult gets $250/month deposited into their ARA regardless of income. When annual income exceeds $30,000, 2-3% is automatically deducted as repayment until lifetime advances are recovered. Market gains on ALL funds—advanced or repaid—belong entirely to you. No interest accrues on 'owed' amounts. Repayment pauses automatically during low-income periods. At death, any unrepaid balance is forgiven. The government essentially provides zero-interest, growth-sharing loans for retirement, recoverable only from income surpluses.",
  "what_is_new": "Retirement contributions happen TO you continuously, not BY you intermittently. The lender keeps none of the investment upside—only recovers principal. Repayment triggers on income, not time, making irregular earning irrelevant.",
  "why_it_works": "Lost compound time is the true cost of income volatility. A $3,000 contribution at age 25 becomes ~$50,000 at 65; at age 45 it becomes ~$12,000. Pre-funding ensures everyone captures early growth. Society recoups its outlay through repayments while recipients keep the multiplicative gains—a positive-sum trade.",
  "why_it_fails": "Requires enormous upfront government capital before repayment streams mature—potentially decades of negative cash flow. Creates intergenerational transfer from current taxpayers to future retirees. Could be exploited by high earners who strategically delay income recognition. Politically vulnerable to 'welfare' framing despite being structured as recoverable investment.",
  "medium_term": "Gig workers, artists, and caregivers accumulate retirement wealth for the first time. Labor market flexibility increases as retirement anxiety decreases. Poverty among elderly irregular earners drops significantly.",
  "long_term_vision": "Retirement security becomes truly universal—disconnected from employment stability, income consistency, or financial sophistication. The link between 'steady job' and 'secure old age' breaks entirely. Irregular work becomes a genuine lifestyle choice rather than a path to elderly poverty. Society captures the full creative and entrepreneurial potential of people no longer terrified of income volatility."
}
```

solution.label: Advance Retirement Accounts (ARAs)
solution.design_principles: The system contributes first; you repay when able. Inconsistency expected, not penalized. Capture compound time regardless of cash flow. Separate contribution timing from growth timing entirely.
solution.core_mechanism: Government continuously funds your retirement account at a base rate. When you earn above a threshold, income-linked deductions 'repay' advances. You keep all market gains; they recover only inflation-adjusted principal.
solution.how_it_works: Every adult gets $250/month deposited into their ARA regardless of income. When annual income exceeds $30,000, 2-3% is automatically deducted as repayment until lifetime advances are recovered. Market gains on ALL funds—advanced or repaid—belong entirely to you. No interest accrues on 'owed' amounts. Repayment pauses automatically during low-income periods. At death, any unrepaid balance is forgiven. The government essentially provides zero-interest, growth-sharing loans for retirement, recoverable only from income surpluses.
solution.what_is_new: Retirement contributions happen TO you continuously, not BY you intermittently. The lender keeps none of the investment upside—only recovers principal. Repayment triggers on income, not time, making irregular earning irrelevant.
solution.why_it_works: Lost compound time is the true cost of income volatility. A $3,000 contribution at age 25 becomes ~$50,000 at 65; at age 45 it becomes ~$12,000. Pre-funding ensures everyone captures early growth. Society recoups its outlay through repayments while recipients keep the multiplicative gains—a positive-sum trade.
solution.why_it_fails: Requires enormous upfront government capital before repayment streams mature—potentially decades of negative cash flow. Creates intergenerational transfer from current taxpayers to future retirees. Could be exploited by high earners who strategically delay income recognition. Politically vulnerable to 'welfare' framing despite being structured as recoverable investment.
solution.medium_term: Gig workers, artists, and caregivers accumulate retirement wealth for the first time. Labor market flexibility increases as retirement anxiety decreases. Poverty among elderly irregular earners drops significantly.
solution.long_term_vision: Retirement security becomes truly universal—disconnected from employment stability, income consistency, or financial sophistication. The link between 'steady job' and 'secure old age' breaks entirely. Irregular work becomes a genuine lifestyle choice rather than a path to elderly poverty. Society captures the full creative and entrepreneurial potential of people no longer terrified of income volatility.
